topic: Australia road scaffold validation — EXP-240 citation retraction and parity reframe
change_summary: EXP-240 integration: retracts unsupported sub-1%/single-free-parameter circuity quantifier in detail_evidence H3; re-characterizes Giacomin & Levinson 2015 as descriptive circuity and Huber & Rust 2016 as OSRM Stata tooling; adds DOI links to both; adds in-sample parity reframe noting V13 10.2% is also in-sample so the comparison holds on both sides.
our_argument_summary: WIN-069 claims V13 two-zone topology + aetheric refraction validates Australian road distances (6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE, 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error; AGD66→GDA94 as topology evidence). Our review (verdict Misleading) argues: (1) road excess over geodesic is a terrain property, not topology; (2) 6.2% RMSE is ~5 orders of magnitude worse than routine sub-m geodesy; (3) AGD66→GDA94 is standard regional→geocentric datum evolution, not topology evidence; (4) V13 parameters (a = π×R_Earth, H(r) fits) are calibrated against globe data, so the 'validation' is circular; (5) terrain-based road-network analysis outperforms V13 on road-distance prediction. The c6 state reflects EXP-240, which retracted the unsupported sub-1%/single-free-parameter quantifier and reframed the comparison on in-sample parity grounds.

relevant_files: raw-text/monitor.py (WIN-069 domain search — no validation logic)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-069 has no monitor.py logic (monitoring = none). The dome publishes the 6.2% and 10.2% as pre-computed numbers derived from a chosen subset of 12 roads + 9 cross-links. V13 parameters (disc radius, refraction profile H(r)) are calibrated against globe distances.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Not applicable — no automation. The 'validation' is a static published result.
data_sources_used: Dome-selected Australian road subset (no disclosed selection criteria)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: 12-road + 9-cross-link sample size; no pre-registration; no out-of-sample test.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our review correctly notes monitor.py has no WIN-069 validation, and correctly identifies V13 parameters as globe-calibrated.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py does not contain a WIN-069 domain or any load/validation logic for Australian road data. c5 and prior cycles confirmed this.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard physics does not claim road distances exceed geodesics by dome-derived amounts; the dome invents a distinct geometric claim. The rebuttal is that the claim is circular (globe-calibrated), not a rename.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: V13 topology and aetheric refraction parameters are calibrated against globe distances already measured (AGD66/GDA94 framework). The predicted distances are effectively fit to the data they 'predict.' No pre-registered out-of-sample test exists.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: V13 is a dome-model construct (two-zone topology + H(r) refractive profile); the predicted road distances are mathematically derived from those dome parameters (even if those parameters are themselves fit to globe measurements).

holes_found.description: Retraction is explicit on 'sub-1%' and 'single free parameter' but the replacement sentence still asserts 'single-digit-percent residuals ... routine in terrain-based road-network analysis' without an explicit citation. Both cited papers (G&L 2015 descriptive circuity; H&R 2016 OSRM tooling intro) support the surrounding context — G&L reports mean circuities ~1.19 empirically, H&R introduces the osrmtime command — but neither paper directly validates 'single-digit-percent residuals against observed road distances' as a predictive-model result. A strict cite-checking dome defender could argue: 'You retracted the stronger quantifier but the weaker one is still uncited — the retraction is incomplete.' The argument is operationally defensible (OSRM uses the actual road graph, so recovered route distances vs. observed will be single-digit-percent by construction for well-mapped regions), but operational defensibility is not a citation. Low severity because (a) the quantifier is much weaker than before, (b) the surviving comparison ('V13 same-hemisphere mean error is 10.2% with ≥4 free parameters, several times worse than the single-digit-percent in-sample residuals that are routine') does not hinge on a specific numeric residual — only on V13 being several times worse than routine-road-network-analysis baselines, and (c) the parity reframe makes the comparison robust regardless.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional low-priority hedge: soften 'routinely produces single-digit-percent residuals against observed road distances' to qualitative phrasing such as 'routinely recovers observed road distances with small residuals (a few percent at most on well-mapped networks)' which avoids a numeric quantifier, OR cite an OSRM validation study explicitly (e.g., Luxen & Vetter 2011 or a downstream OSRM residual paper). No rewrite needed if we are comfortable that 'single-digit-percent' = '<10%' and will be read as qualitative rather than precise.

holes_found.description: Carryover from c5 hole #5 and c4: detail_evidence final paragraph still says 'thousands of possible Australian road pairs' — this remains argumentatively correct but gives a defender a number to haggle with. Explicitly deferred in c4/c5 as low-priority. No change since the cycle-5 patch scope did not touch this.
holes_found.recommendation: Unchanged from c5: next minor WIN-069 cleanup EXP should replace 'thousands of possible Australian road pairs' with a defensible characterization such as 'the larger set of major transcontinental and inter-city Australian road pairs' or a bounded count (e.g., '~50 inter-city pairs among Australia 15 largest cities').

holes_found.description: Carryover from c4/c5 minor hole: the 90-Mile Straight '<0.4%' figure is ~40× looser than the true WGS84 geodesic/chord separation (which is well under 0.01% on a 146.6 km segment). Loose but not wrong; matches the TLDR. Deferred as low-priority in c4 and c5.
holes_found.recommendation: Unchanged: tighten '<0.4%' to '<0.01%' in detail_evidence and TLDRs on the next WIN-069 micro-pass.

stronger_arguments.description: If an explicit citation for single-digit-percent OSRM residuals is desired, Luxen & Vetter (2011, ACM SIGSPATIAL GIS, 'Real-time routing with OpenStreetMap data,' DOI 10.1145/2093973.2094062) is the canonical OSRM reference and describes the routing engine design. Boeing (2019, 'The morphology and circuity of walkable and drivable street networks') and Levinson & El-Geneidy (2009, 'The minimum circuity frontier,' Regional Science and Urban Economics 39(6):732-738) provide empirical circuity fits that would directly support quantitative residual claims if that level of precision were needed.
sources: Luxen & Vetter 2011, ACM SIGSPATIAL GIS, DOI 10.1145/2093973.2094062
Boeing 2019, Environment and Planning B: Urban Analytics and City Science

stronger_arguments.description: The parity reframe is sound but under-emphasized. It would be even stronger to state explicitly: 'Both V13 and the OSRM+circuity baseline use in-sample fits. The circuity/OSRM framework uses fewer parameters (a mean detour index + road graph topology) and still outperforms V13 10.2% same-hemisphere error. Matching parameter budgets makes the comparison more damning, not less.' This is implicit in the current text but could be made explicit.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies that road distances systematically exceed great-circle (geodesic) distances across transcontinental Australia, and correctly identifies that AGD66 was non-geocentric. Both are facts. The V13 topology two-zone construction is a creative attempt to model this excess.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Both facts are explained by standard physics/geodesy without invoking dome topology. Road excess is a terrain and network-circuity property (documented across every continent); AGD66→GDA94 is a routine regional→geocentric datum transition that every continent performed. The dome V13 prediction is calibrated against globe distances, making the match circular. None of the three observations (road excess, 6.2% RMSE, datum shift) discriminates between dome topology and standard terrain-plus-geocentric-geodesy.

deeper_analysis:
EXP-240 successfully addresses the two moderate c5 findings (ISS-1487 citation-quantifier integrity and ISS-1488 circularity). Web-verified both DOIs: (1) Giacomin & Levinson 2015, 'Road network circuity in metropolitan areas,' Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design 42(6):1040-1053, DOI 10.1068/b130131p — characterized correctly as descriptive circuity. Paper measures circuity across 51 US MSAs 1990-2010, reports mean circuity ~1.19 with terrain- and vintage-related variation. No predictive-model residuals reported (consistent with EXP-240 retraction). (2) Huber & Rust 2016, 'Calculate Travel Time and Distance with Openstreetmap Data Using the Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM),' The Stata Journal 16(2):416-423, DOI 10.1177/1536867X1601600209 — characterized correctly as OSRM Stata command tooling paper. The paper introduces osrmtime for computing distances and travel times via OSRM; it does not report predictive residuals. The retraction scope is accurate: both papers are cited for what they actually provide (circuity range context + routing tool), and the 'sub-1%' / 'single free parameter' quantifier is explicitly abandoned.

The parity reframe resolves ISS-1488 circularity concern cleanly. The old framing invited the defender counterpunch ('you are demanding out-of-sample from us but comparing us to an in-sample fit'). The new framing concedes the in-sample nature of both sides and argues on parameter budget and residual magnitude instead: V13 has ≥4 free parameters and 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error; routine road-network analysis has fewer parameters and single-digit-percent residuals. V13 loses on both axes.

The one residual hole — the single-digit-percent quantifier — is substantially weaker than the retracted sub-1%. A strict cite-checker could flag it, but it reads more like a qualitative statement than a precision claim, and the surviving comparison ('V13 is several times worse than routine terrain-based analysis') does not depend on the specific number. Optional cleanup via softer phrasing or an explicit OSRM validation citation.

No verdict change. 'Misleading' continues to hold: the WIN is presented as validation of dome topology, but the evidence is (a) a terrain-driven geographic fact badly fit by the dome, (b) a circularly calibrated geometric claim, and (c) a misreading of datum-evolution history. The detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text continue to support this verdict.

TLDR check: tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict both accurately summarize the detail content without contradiction. Neither was touched by EXP-240 and neither needs a patch.

citations_verified: Giacomin & Levinson 2015, 'Road network circuity in metropolitan areas,' Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design 42(6):1040-1053, DOI 10.1068/b130131p — verified via web search. Paper confirmed as descriptive circuity across 51 US MSAs 1990-2010. Characterization as 'descriptive circuity' literature is accurate; mean circuity ~1.19 is the reported empirical figure.

Huber & Rust 2016, 'Calculate Travel Time and Distance with Openstreetmap Data Using the Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM),' The Stata Journal 16(2):416-423, DOI 10.1177/1536867X1601600209 — verified via web search. Paper confirmed as osrmtime Stata command tooling intro. Characterization as 'OSRM Stata command tooling' is accurate.
AGD66 non-geocentric design (usq.pressbooks.pub/gpsandgnss/chapter/2-2-australian-datums/) — unchanged from c4/c5 verification.
Wikipedia GDA94 entry — unchanged from prior verification.
a = 20,015 km ≈ π × 6,371 km — arithmetic check OK.
~250,000× / 5 orders of magnitude arithmetic (6.2% × 4,000 km = 248 km; 248 km / 1 m = 2.48 × 10^5) — OK.
citations_unchecked: Internal anchor #posthoc-standard (Part 3.1b) — depends on built HTML.
Internal anchor #timestamp-error — depends on built HTML.
Kill-Shot #1 internal cross-reference in detail_verdict_text — depends on section-rendering state.

'single-digit-percent residuals ... routine in terrain-based road-network analysis' — qualitative claim not explicitly cited to a validation paper (flagged as minor hole above). Would need Luxen & Vetter 2011 or Boeing 2019 if a strict citation is desired.
